title.en: Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct: Build a Private Multimodal Assistant Pipeline

description.en: Turn Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct into a structured multimodal assistant that extracts data from images, performs OCR on documents, and returns machine-readable JSON â€” all running locally on consumer hardware with full privacy.

story_variants.en:
## From Pixels to Structured Data: Your Local Vision Assistant

Vaathiyaar here! Describing an image is cool, but what if your local AI could *extract structured data* from a photo â€” pull line items from a receipt, parse fields from a form, or return bounding-box descriptions as JSON? That's the power of an **instruction-tuned** vision-language model. Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct doesn't just see images; it follows precise instructions about *how* to respond, making it perfect for building reliable assistant pipelines on your own machine.

### Instruction Tuning: Why It Matters

| Base Model | Instruct Model |
|---|---|
| Predicts next token generically | Follows user instructions faithfully |
| Output format is unpredictable | Can output JSON, lists, tables on request |
| Needs few-shot examples | Works zero-shot with clear prompts |
| Good for fine-tuning | Good for direct deployment |
| Raw text completion | Chat-ready with system/user/assistant roles |

The `-Instruct` suffix means Qwen3-VL-2B was further trained on instruction-following datasets, so it reliably obeys formatting constraints â€” critical for building pipelines that downstream code can parse.

### Setting Up the Pipeline

```python
from transformers import Qwen3VLForConditionalGeneration, AutoProcessor

model_name = "Qwen/Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct"
processor = AutoProcessor.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = Qwen3VLForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained(
    model_name, torch_dtype="auto", device_map="auto"
)
```

This downloads ~4GB of weights once and caches them. With `device_map='auto'`, the model uses your GPU if available, falling back to CPU.

### Structured Extraction from Images

The key to reliable extraction is a well-crafted system prompt:

```python
from PIL import Image

image = Image.open("receipt.jpg")

messages = [
    {"role": "system", "content": "You are a document parser. Always respond with valid JSON."},
    {"role": "user", "content": [
        {"type": "image", "image": image},
        {"type": "text", "text": "Extract all line items as a JSON array with keys: item, quantity, price."}
    ]}
]

text_prompt = processor.apply_chat_template(
    messages, tokenize=False, add_generation_prompt=True
)
inputs = processor(
    text=[text_prompt], images=[image],
    return_tensors="pt"
).to(model.device)

output = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=500, temperature=0.1)
result = processor.decode(
    output[0][inputs["input_ids"].shape[-1]:], skip_special_tokens=True
)
print(result)
```

Using `temperature=0.1` keeps the output deterministic â€” essential when parsing the result as JSON downstream.

### Building a Reusable Assistant Class

```python
import json

class VisionAssistant:
    def __init__(self, model, processor, system_prompt=""):
        self.model = model
        self.processor = processor
        self.system_prompt = system_prompt

    def analyze(self, image, question, max_tokens=300):
        messages = []
        if self.system_prompt:
            messages.append({"role": "system", "content": self.system_prompt})
        messages.append({"role": "user", "content": [
            {"type": "image", "image": image},
            {"type": "text", "text": question}
        ]})
        prompt = self.processor.apply_chat_template(
            messages, tokenize=False, add_generation_prompt=True
        )
        inputs = self.processor(
            text=[prompt], images=[image], return_tensors="pt"
        ).to(self.model.device)
        out = self.model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=max_tokens, temperature=0.1)
        return self.processor.decode(
            out[0][inputs["input_ids"].shape[-1]:], skip_special_tokens=True
        )

assistant = VisionAssistant(model, processor, system_prompt="Respond in JSON.")
```

### Privacy and Performance

| Metric | Cloud API | Local Qwen3-VL-2B |
|---|---|---|
| Data privacy | Images sent externally | Never leaves your machine |
| Cost per image | $0.01 - $0.05 | Free after download |
| Latency | 1-3s (network + inference) | 0.5-2s (inference only) |
| Offline capable | No | Yes |
| Output control | Limited | Full prompt engineering |

> **Key Insight:** Instruction-tuned vision models like Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct bridge the gap between 'AI that sees' and 'AI that does useful work with what it sees.' By combining system prompts, low temperature, and structured output requests, you transform a general-purpose VLM into a reliable, private data extraction pipeline â€” no cloud required.

animation_sequence.content: Vision-language models can do more than describe pictures. Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct follows precise instructions to extract structured data from images â€” parse receipts, read forms, analyze charts â€” and return clean JSON. All running locally on ~5GB VRAM with zero cloud dependencies.

animation_sequence.code:
from transformers import Qwen3VLForConditionalGeneration, AutoProcessor
from PIL import Image

model_name = "Qwen/Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct"
processor = AutoProcessor.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = Qwen3VLForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained(
    model_name, torch_dtype="auto", device_map="auto"
)

image = Image.open("receipt.jpg")

messages = [
    {"role": "system", "content": "You are a document parser. Always respond with valid JSON."},
    {"role": "user", "content": [
        {"type": "image", "image": image},
        {"type": "text", "text": "Extract all line items as JSON with keys: item, quantity, price."}
    ]}
]

text_prompt = processor.apply_chat_template(
    messages, tokenize=False, add_generation_prompt=True
)
inputs = processor(
    text=[text_prompt], images=[image],
    return_tensors="pt"
).to(model.device)

output = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=500, temperature=0.1)
result = processor.decode(
    output[0][inputs["input_ids"].shape[-1]:], skip_special_tokens=True
)
print(result)

steps.explanation: Import Qwen3VLForConditionalGeneration â€” the model class for Qwen3's vision-language architecture â€” and AutoProcessor for unified text+image preprocessing.
PIL (Pillow) provides the Image class. Qwen3-VL expects PIL Image objects as visual input.
Point to the instruction-tuned variant on Hugging Face. The '-Instruct' suffix means it follows formatting instructions reliably.
The processor handles both tokenization and image preprocessing â€” resizing, normalization, and converting pixels to tensors.
Load ~4GB of model weights. torch_dtype='auto' picks optimal precision (bfloat16 on supported GPUs); device_map='auto' distributes across available hardware.
Load a local image file. In production this could be a scanned document, screenshot, or camera capture.
The system message instructs the model to always output JSON â€” this instruction-tuned model will follow this constraint reliably.
The user message combines an image content block with a text block containing the extraction instruction. The model sees both simultaneously.
apply_chat_template converts the structured messages into the special token format Qwen3-VL was trained on, handling image placeholder tokens.
Generate with temperature=0.1 for deterministic, parseable output. Low temperature reduces creativity but increases format reliability â€” exactly what data extraction needs.
Wrap the model and processor in a reusable class. The system_prompt parameter lets you configure behavior per use case â€” JSON parser, OCR engine, chart reader.
Track analysis history for logging, debugging, or building multi-step pipelines where later steps reference earlier results.
The analyze method takes a PIL Image and a question string, handling all the template formatting and generation internally.
Conditionally prepend the system prompt. This lets the same class work with or without formatting constraints.
Build the multimodal user message with image and text content blocks â€” the standard format for Qwen3-VL's chat interface.
Generate with temperature=0.1 by default. For a production pipeline, deterministic output means consistent, parseable responses.
Because we used a JSON system prompt, the response should be valid JSON. json.loads() parses it into a Python dict or list for downstream processing.

items.left: Better for fine-tuning on custom data

animation_sequence.message: You've built a private vision assistant pipeline! Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct extracts structured data from images on your own hardware â€” no cloud, no cost, no data leakage.

instruction.en: Build a `StructuredVisionPipeline` class that simulates a vision-language model pipeline for extracting structured data from images. Initialize with `system_prompt` (str) and `temperature` (float, 0.0-2.0 inclusive, raises ValueError otherwise). Implement `extract(image_path, instruction)` which returns a dict with keys 'image', 'instruction', 'system_prompt', 'temperature', and 'output'. The 'output' value should be: if the instruction contains 'json' (case-insensitive), return '{"extracted": true}'; if it contains 'list', return '- item 1\n- item 2'; otherwise return 'Analysis complete.'. Also implement `batch_extract(image_paths, instruction)` that calls extract for each path and returns a list of results, and `get_stats()` returning a dict with 'total_calls' (int) and 'unique_images' (number of distinct image paths processed).

practice_challenges.starter_code:
class StructuredVisionPipeline:
    def __init__(self, system_prompt: str = "", temperature: float = 0.1):
        # Validate temperature: 0.0 to 2.0 inclusive
        # Store system_prompt, temperature
        # Track calls and unique images
        pass

    def extract(self, image_path: str, instruction: str) -> dict:
        # Return dict with: image, instruction, system_prompt, temperature, output
        # output depends on instruction content (json/list/other)
        pass

    def batch_extract(self, image_paths: list, instruction: str) -> list:
        # Call extract for each image_path, return list of results
        pass

    def get_stats(self) -> dict:
        # Return total_calls and unique_images count
        pass

# Test it:
pipe = StructuredVisionPipeline(system_prompt="Parse documents", temperature=0.1)
r1 = pipe.extract("receipt.jpg", "Extract items as JSON")
print(r1["output"])
r2 = pipe.extract("menu.jpg", "List all dishes")
print(r2["output"])
r3 = pipe.extract("receipt.jpg", "Describe this image")
print(r3["output"])
stats = pipe.get_stats()
print(stats["total_calls"])
print(stats["unique_images"])

en: Use a set to track unique image paths â€” self.unique_images = set() â€” and add each image_path in extract()
Check 'json' in instruction.lower() first, then 'list' in instruction.lower(), then fall through to 'Analysis complete.'
batch_extract is simply: return [self.extract(path, instruction) for path in image_paths] â€” each call updates stats automatically

practice_challenges.test_code:
pipe = StructuredVisionPipeline()
assert pipe.get_stats() == {"total_calls": 0, "unique_images": 0}
r = pipe.extract("a.jpg", "Return JSON")
assert r["output"] == '{"extracted": true}'
assert r["image"] == "a.jpg"
assert r["temperature"] == 0.1
r2 = pipe.extract("b.jpg", "Give me a list")
assert r2["output"] == "- item 1\n- item 2"
r3 = pipe.extract("a.jpg", "Summarize")
assert r3["output"] == "Analysis complete."
assert pipe.get_stats() == {"total_calls": 3, "unique_images": 2}
batch = pipe.batch_extract(["c.jpg", "d.jpg"], "Return JSON")
assert len(batch) == 2
assert batch[0]["output"] == '{"extracted": true}'
assert pipe.get_stats()["total_calls"] == 5
assert pipe.get_stats()["unique_images"] == 4
try:
    StructuredVisionPipeline(temperature=3.0)
    assert False
except ValueError:
    pass
try:
    StructuredVisionPipeline(temperature=-0.1)
    assert False
except ValueError:
    pass
pipe2 = StructuredVisionPipeline(system_prompt="test")
assert pipe2.extract("x.jpg", "hi")["system_prompt"] == "test"

instruction.en: Build a `MultimodalMessage` class that constructs and validates chat messages for a vision-language model. Initialize with `role` (must be 'system', 'user', or 'assistant' â€” raise ValueError otherwise). Implement `add_text(text)` to append a text content block, `add_image(path)` to append an image content block (raises ValueError if role is 'system' or 'assistant'), `get_content()` which returns a list of content block dicts (each with 'type' and either 'text' or 'path' key), and `to_dict()` returning {'role': role, 'content': content_list}. If only one text block exists and no image blocks, `to_dict` should simplify content to just the text string instead of a list.

practice_challenges.expected_output:
image
What is in this image?
<class 'str'>
Invalid role caught

en: Store content blocks as a list of dicts. add_text appends {'type': 'text', 'text': text}, add_image appends {'type': 'image', 'path': path}
In add_image, check self.role != 'user' and raise ValueError â€” only user messages can contain images in the chat format
In to_dict, check if len(self.content) == 1 and self.content[0]['type'] == 'text' â€” if so, return the text string directly instead of the list

practice_challenges.test_code:
msg = MultimodalMessage("user")
msg.add_text("hello")
msg.add_image("img.jpg")
d = msg.to_dict()
assert d["role"] == "user"
assert isinstance(d["content"], list)
assert len(d["content"]) == 2
assert d["content"][0]["type"] == "text"
assert d["content"][1]["type"] == "image"
assert d["content"][1]["path"] == "img.jpg"

sys = MultimodalMessage("system")
sys.add_text("Be helpful")
assert isinstance(sys.to_dict()["content"], str)
assert sys.to_dict()["content"] == "Be helpful"

try:
    sys.add_image("x.jpg")
    assert False
except ValueError:
    pass

try:
    MultimodalMessage("tool")
    assert False
except ValueError:
    pass

assistant_msg = MultimodalMessage("assistant")
try:
    assistant_msg.add_image("x.jpg")
    assert False
except ValueError:
    pass
assistant_msg.add_text("I see a cat")
assert assistant_msg.to_dict()["content"] == "I see a cat"

multi = MultimodalMessage("user")
multi.add_text("a")
multi.add_text("b")
assert isinstance(multi.to_dict()["content"], list)
assert len(multi.to_dict()["content"]) == 2
